title: Paraneoplastic Syndromes
oneLiner: Remote, non-metastatic effects of malignancy; often present before cancer diagnosis; early recognition stops disability and finds a hidden tumour.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe hypercalcaemia (Ca >3.5 mmol/L)** → cardiac arrest, coma, or acute kidney injury. *Single finding:* short QT interval on ECG + dehydration + confusion.
- **Myasthenic / Lambert-Eaton crisis** → respiratory failure from diaphragmatic weakness. *Single finding:* fatigable ptosis/diplopia + proximal weakness + dropping SpO₂ on exertion or supine.
- **Severe hyponatraemia (Na <120, from SIADH)** → seizures, reduced GCS. *Single finding:* AVP-inappropriate urine osmolality > plasma osmolality + low serum urate.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - **Neurological:** proximal muscle weakness (LEMS), ptosis/diplopia (MG), ataxia (cerebellar degeneration), sensory neuropathy, limbic encephalitis (short-term memory loss, seizures, personality change)
  - **Endocrine:** weight loss, hypertension/hypokalaemia (ectopic ACTH), polyuria/polydipsia (SIADH), flushes (carcinoid)
  - **Dermatological:** rash in sun-exposed areas + Gottron’s papules (dermatomyositis), acanthosis nigricans, necrolytic migratory erythema (glucagonoma)
  - **Haematological:** migratory thrombophlebitis (Trousseau’s), erythrocytosis (renal cell cancer)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **LEMS:** proximal weakness, hyporeflexia, post-exercise facilitation (reflex returns briefly)
  - **MG:** fatigable weakness – ptosis on upgaze, diplopia on lateral gaze, normal reflexes
  - **Dermatomyositis:** heliotrope rash (eyelids), Gottron’s sign (knuckle papules), periungual telangiectasias
  - **Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy:** clubbing + periostitis (lung cancer)
  - **Cushing’s:** moon face, proximal myopathy, bruises, striae
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Myasthenia gravis** (thymoma) → **vs** **LEMS** (small cell lung cancer). *Splitter:* reflex facilitation after brief exercise (LEMS) vs normal/post-tetanic exhaustion (MG). Autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, impotence) tips to LEMS.
  - **SIADH** (lung, CNS tumours) → **vs** **other hyponatraemia** (heart failure, cirrhosis, diuretics). *Splitter:* low urate + low urea + high urine Na >40 → paraneoplastic SIADH. Hypothyroidism/adrenal insufficiency also cause hyponatraemia.
  - **Dermatomyositis** (ovarian, lung, GI cancers) → **vs** **polymyositis** (no rash) or drug-induced myositis (statins). *Splitter:* presence of Gottron’s / heliotrope rash.
  - **Hypercalcaemia** (squamous cell lung, breast, myeloma, PTHrP) → **vs** **primary hyperparathyroidism**. *Splitter:* low phosphate, high PTHrP, low 1,25-vitamin D in malignancy; high PTH in primary.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (short QT in hyperCa)
  - Spirometry / single breath count (low in MG/LEMS→ need NIF/MIP)
  - Urine dipstick, urine osmolality (SIADH work-up)
  - Pregnancy test in women of childbearing age
- **Bloods:**
  - Routine: FBC, U&E, LFT, Ca²⁺, PO₄³⁻, Mg²⁺, glucose, TSH, cortisol, ACTH
  - Paraneoplastic antibodies: anti-Hu (encephalitis, sensory neuropathy), anti-Yo (cerebellar), anti-Ri (opsoclonus), anti-Ma2 (limbic), anti-VGCC (LEMS), anti-AChR (MG), anti-TIF1γ (dermatomyositis + cancer risk)
  - Tumour markers: CEA, CA125, NSE, AFP, hCG (as guided by clinical suspicion)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (whole body screen)
  - Second-line: PET-CT (occult primary), MRI brain (paraneoplastic encephalitis / cerebellar degeneration)
- **Special tests:**
  - EMG / NCS: in LEMS → decrement at low frequency, increment after brief exercise or high-frequency stimulation
  - Skin / muscle biopsy (dermatomyositis)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **PNS Care 2004:** definite (classical syndrome + cancer within 5 years or high-titre well-characterised antibody), probable, possible.
- **Never-miss test:** **Contrast-enhanced CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis** — if you don’t look for the tumour, you treat symptoms and miss the cancer.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Hypercalcaemia >3.0:** give 0.9% saline 500 mL stat, repeat until euvolaemic; consider zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over ≥15 min (check renal function) or pamidronate 90 mg IV over 2 h.
  - **Suspected myasthenic/LEMS crisis:** senior + anaesthetics; prepare for non-invasive ventilation; if known MG, IVIG or plasma exchange [check local protocol]. Do NOT give suxamethonium.
  - **Hyponatraemia with seizures:** hypertonic saline (3%) 100 mL bolus over 10 min, aim for 5 mEq/L rise in Na; call senior.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Treat underlying cancer** → refer to oncology asap (surgery, chemo/radiotherapy often improves the paraneoplastic syndrome).
  - **Symptomatic:** for LEMS → 3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) [check local dose], or pyridostigmine; for MG → pyridostigmine 30–60 mg PO QDS + prednisolone [taper under specialist].
  - **Paraneoplastic encephalitis / neuropathy:** IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days, or methylprednisolone 1 g IV × 3 days [check local policy]. Consider rituximab or cyclophosphamide if refractory.
  - **Dermatomyositis:** prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day PO, slowly taper.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete oncological treatment (surgery / radiotherapy / chemotherapy).
  - Neurological or rheumatology follow-up for residual deficits.
  - Consider tumour surveillance imaging every 6–12 months if no primary found (occult).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 1–2 mL/kg/h (avoid free water if SIADH).
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless active bleeding, thrombocytopenia, or CNS tumour.
  - Diet: normal unless NBM for procedures.
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, 12-lead ECG if hyperCa, daily U&E/Ca.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Hypercalcaemia: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV single dose (check eGFR >35) or pamidronate 90 mg IV; calcitonin [not first-line in UK, never first dose without senior].
  - LEMS: 3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) – [check local policy] typical starting dose 5–10 mg PO QDS.
  - MG: pyridostigmine 30–60 mg PO QDS (max 120 mg QDS); prednisolone 20–40 mg daily (start low if respiratory impairment).
  - IVIG: dose 0.4 g/kg/day IV × 5 days (round to nearest vial).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Azathioprine (2.5 mg/kg/day) or mycophenolate for chronic MG.
  - Bisphosphonate second dose in hyperCa: give after 7 days if Ca still high.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Zoledronic acid: avoid if eGFR <35, not in pregnancy, risk of renal failure if hypovolaemic.
  - IVIG: check IgA levels (risk of anaphylaxis in IgA deficiency).
  - Pyridostigmine: caution in asthma, cardiac arrhythmias (increases vagal tone).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Bisphosphonates: reduce dose if CrCl <35 (or avoid). Consider [check local policy].
  - IVIG: no dose adjustment for renal, but monitor creatinine.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Hypertonic saline** – *correcting Na too fast* (>10 mEq/L in 24 h) → osmotic demyelination. Check Na every 2–4 h.
  - **Prednisolone in myasthenia** – *starting high dose* (e.g., 60 mg) can cause temporary worsening → risk of myasthenic crisis. Always start low (20 mg) and increase slowly.
  - **IVIG** – *overly fast infusion* leads to headache, thrombosis, acute kidney injury. Infuse over 4–6 h with pre-hydration.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure from NMJ weakness (MG/LEMS) → intubation.
  - Severe hypercalcaemia → acute renal failure, pancreatitis, ECG changes, cardiac arrest.
  - Hyponatraemia → seizures, coma, osmotic demyelination with over-correction.
  - Thrombosis (Trousseau’s) → PE, CVA.
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent cerebellar ataxia (anti-Yo), sensory ataxia (anti-Hu) – may not reverse even after tumour treatment.
  - Immunosuppression from long-term steroids/immunosuppressants.
  - Recurrence of paraneoplastic syndrome if tumour relapses.
sections.Prognosis:
- Depends entirely on the underlying cancer – complete tumour removal often improves the paraneoplastic syndrome (except some neurological syndromes like anti-Hu which can be permanent).
- Overall survival reflects the cancer stage. Paraneoplastic neurological syndromes with small cell lung cancer have median survival ~11–15 months (worse if anti-Hu).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check calcium, sodium, LDH, and urine osmolality** in a patient with unexplained weight loss and confusion or weakness.
- **Perform a 60-second bedside neurological screen** – fatigue on upgaze (MG) or reflex facilitation (LEMS) can clinch the diagnosis.
- **Order a whole-body CT** in any new neurological syndrome without an obvious cause – the cancer is often small and hidden.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t dismiss proximal weakness as ‘deconditioning’** in an elderly patient – check reflexes and look for fatigability.
- **Don’t give high-dose steroids for suspected encephalitis without first checking for infection** (HSV, VZV, mycoplasma) – can worsen sepsis.
- **Don’t give neostigmine or suxamethonium** in suspected LEMS – LEMS is hypersensitive to depolarising agents (prolonged paralysis).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New difficulty swallowing, slurred speech, drooping eyelids, or shortness of breath.
  - New confusion, seizure, or headache with nausea.
  - Worsening weakness – especially unable to walk up stairs or brush hair.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urgent neurology, rheumatology, or oncology outpatient appointment (within 2 weeks).
  - Repeat imaging in 3–6 months if no primary found.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / anaesthetics / crash team if:**
  - SpO₂ <92% on room air, NIF <–30 cmH₂O, or single breath count <15.
  - GCS ≤12, new seizures, or Na <115 or Ca >3.5 mmol/L.
  - NEWS2 ≥6 (especially respiratory component ≥2 and rising).
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or pH <7.3 in setting of weakness.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Hypercalcaemia >3.0 → inform senior, call oncology.
  - Myasthenic crisis: any sign of hypoventilation → call ITU/pain team (neostigmine can be used but only after senior review).
  - SIADH with Na <120 → discuss with endocrine specialist before any fluid or DDAVP.

rawMarkdown:
## Paraneoplastic Syndromes
**One-liner:** Remote, non-metastatic effects of malignancy; often present before cancer diagnosis; early recognition stops disability and finds a hidden tumour.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe hypercalcaemia (Ca >3.5 mmol/L)** → cardiac arrest, coma, or acute kidney injury. *Single finding:* short QT interval on ECG + dehydration + confusion.
- **Myasthenic / Lambert-Eaton crisis** → respiratory failure from diaphragmatic weakness. *Single finding:* fatigable ptosis/diplopia + proximal weakness + dropping SpO₂ on exertion or supine.
- **Severe hyponatraemia (Na <120, from SIADH)** → seizures, reduced GCS. *Single finding:* AVP-inappropriate urine osmolality > plasma osmolality + low serum urate.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - **Neurological:** proximal muscle weakness (LEMS), ptosis/diplopia (MG), ataxia (cerebellar degeneration), sensory neuropathy, limbic encephalitis (short-term memory loss, seizures, personality change)
  - **Endocrine:** weight loss, hypertension/hypokalaemia (ectopic ACTH), polyuria/polydipsia (SIADH), flushes (carcinoid)
  - **Dermatological:** rash in sun-exposed areas + Gottron’s papules (dermatomyositis), acanthosis nigricans, necrolytic migratory erythema (glucagonoma)
  - **Haematological:** migratory thrombophlebitis (Trousseau’s), erythrocytosis (renal cell cancer)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **LEMS:** proximal weakness, hyporeflexia, post-exercise facilitation (reflex returns briefly)
  - **MG:** fatigable weakness – ptosis on upgaze, diplopia on lateral gaze, normal reflexes
  - **Dermatomyositis:** heliotrope rash (eyelids), Gottron’s sign (knuckle papules), periungual telangiectasias
  - **Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy:** clubbing + periostitis (lung cancer)
  - **Cushing’s:** moon face, proximal myopathy, bruises, striae

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Myasthenia gravis** (thymoma) → **vs** **LEMS** (small cell lung cancer). *Splitter:* reflex facilitation after brief exercise (LEMS) vs normal/post-tetanic exhaustion (MG). Autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, impotence) tips to LEMS.
  - **SIADH** (lung, CNS tumours) → **vs** **other hyponatraemia** (heart failure, cirrhosis, diuretics). *Splitter:* low urate + low urea + high urine Na >40 → paraneoplastic SIADH. Hypothyroidism/adrenal insufficiency also cause hyponatraemia.
  - **Dermatomyositis** (ovarian, lung, GI cancers) → **vs** **polymyositis** (no rash) or drug-induced myositis (statins). *Splitter:* presence of Gottron’s / heliotrope rash.
  - **Hypercalcaemia** (squamous cell lung, breast, myeloma, PTHrP) → **vs** **primary hyperparathyroidism**. *Splitter:* low phosphate, high PTHrP, low 1,25-vitamin D in malignancy; high PTH in primary.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (short QT in hyperCa)
  - Spirometry / single breath count (low in MG/LEMS→ need NIF/MIP)
  - Urine dipstick, urine osmolality (SIADH work-up)
  - Pregnancy test in women of childbearing age
- **Bloods:**
  - Routine: FBC, U&E, LFT, Ca²⁺, PO₄³⁻, Mg²⁺, glucose, TSH, cortisol, ACTH
  - Paraneoplastic antibodies: anti-Hu (encephalitis, sensory neuropathy), anti-Yo (cerebellar), anti-Ri (opsoclonus), anti-Ma2 (limbic), anti-VGCC (LEMS), anti-AChR (MG), anti-TIF1γ (dermatomyositis + cancer risk)
  - Tumour markers: CEA, CA125, NSE, AFP, hCG (as guided by clinical suspicion)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (whole body screen)
  - Second-line: PET-CT (occult primary), MRI brain (paraneoplastic encephalitis / cerebellar degeneration)
- **Special tests:**
  - EMG / NCS: in LEMS → decrement at low frequency, increment after brief exercise or high-frequency stimulation
  - Skin / muscle biopsy (dermatomyositis)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **PNS Care 2004:** definite (classical syndrome + cancer within 5 years or high-titre well-characterised antibody), probable, possible.
- **Never-miss test:** **Contrast-enhanced CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis** — if you don’t look for the tumour, you treat symptoms and miss the cancer.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Hypercalcaemia >3.0:** give 0.9% saline 500 mL stat, repeat until euvolaemic; consider zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over ≥15 min (check renal function) or pamidronate 90 mg IV over 2 h.
  - **Suspected myasthenic/LEMS crisis:** senior + anaesthetics; prepare for non-invasive ventilation; if known MG, IVIG or plasma exchange [check local protocol]. Do NOT give suxamethonium.
  - **Hyponatraemia with seizures:** hypertonic saline (3%) 100 mL bolus over 10 min, aim for 5 mEq/L rise in Na; call senior.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Treat underlying cancer** → refer to oncology asap (surgery, chemo/radiotherapy often improves the paraneoplastic syndrome).
  - **Symptomatic:** for LEMS → 3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) [check local dose], or pyridostigmine; for MG → pyridostigmine 30–60 mg PO QDS + prednisolone [taper under specialist].
  - **Paraneoplastic encephalitis / neuropathy:** IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days, or methylprednisolone 1 g IV × 3 days [check local policy]. Consider rituximab or cyclophosphamide if refractory.
  - **Dermatomyositis:** prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day PO, slowly taper.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete oncological treatment (surgery / radiotherapy / chemotherapy).
  - Neurological or rheumatology follow-up for residual deficits.
  - Consider tumour surveillance imaging every 6–12 months if no primary found (occult).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 1–2 mL/kg/h (avoid free water if SIADH).
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless active bleeding, thrombocytopenia, or CNS tumour.
  - Diet: normal unless NBM for procedures.
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2, 12-lead ECG if hyperCa, daily U&E/Ca.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Hypercalcaemia: zoledronic acid 4 mg IV single dose (check eGFR >35) or pamidronate 90 mg IV; calcitonin [not first-line in UK, never first dose without senior].
  - LEMS: 3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) – [check local policy] typical starting dose 5–10 mg PO QDS.
  - MG: pyridostigmine 30–60 mg PO QDS (max 120 mg QDS); prednisolone 20–40 mg daily (start low if respiratory impairment).
  - IVIG: dose 0.4 g/kg/day IV × 5 days (round to nearest vial).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Azathioprine (2.5 mg/kg/day) or mycophenolate for chronic MG.
  - Bisphosphonate second dose in hyperCa: give after 7 days if Ca still high.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Zoledronic acid: avoid if eGFR <35, not in pregnancy, risk of renal failure if hypovolaemic.
  - IVIG: check IgA levels (risk of anaphylaxis in IgA deficiency).
  - Pyridostigmine: caution in asthma, cardiac arrhythmias (increases vagal tone).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Bisphosphonates: reduce dose if CrCl <35 (or avoid). Consider [check local policy].
  - IVIG: no dose adjustment for renal, but monitor creatinine.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Hypertonic saline** – *correcting Na too fast* (>10 mEq/L in 24 h) → osmotic demyelination. Check Na every 2–4 h.
  - **Prednisolone in myasthenia** – *starting high dose* (e.g., 60 mg) can cause temporary worsening → risk of myasthenic crisis. Always start low (20 mg) and increase slowly.
  - **IVIG** – *overly fast infusion* leads to headache, thrombosis, acute kidney injury. Infuse over 4–6 h with pre-hydration.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure from NMJ weakness (MG/LEMS) → intubation.
  - Severe hypercalcaemia → acute renal failure, pancreatitis, ECG changes, cardiac arrest.
  - Hyponatraemia → seizures, coma, osmotic demyelination with over-correction.
  - Thrombosis (Trousseau’s) → PE, CVA.
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent cerebellar ataxia (anti-Yo), sensory ataxia (anti-Hu) – may not reverse even after tumour treatment.
  - Immunosuppression from long-term steroids/immunosuppressants.
  - Recurrence of paraneoplastic syndrome if tumour relapses.

### Prognosis
- Depends entirely on the underlying cancer – complete tumour removal often improves the paraneoplastic syndrome (except some neurological syndromes like anti-Hu which can be permanent).
- Overall survival reflects the cancer stage. Paraneoplastic neurological syndromes with small cell lung cancer have median survival ~11–15 months (worse if anti-Hu).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check calcium, sodium, LDH, and urine osmolality** in a patient with unexplained weight loss and confusion or weakness.
- **Perform a 60-second bedside neurological screen** – fatigue on upgaze (MG) or reflex facilitation (LEMS) can clinch the diagnosis.
- **Order a whole-body CT** in any new neurological syndrome without an obvious cause – the cancer is often small and hidden.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t dismiss proximal weakness as ‘deconditioning’** in an elderly patient – check reflexes and look for fatigability.
- **Don’t give high-dose steroids for suspected encephalitis without first checking for infection** (HSV, VZV, mycoplasma) – can worsen sepsis.
- **Don’t give neostigmine or suxamethonium** in suspected LEMS – LEMS is hypersensitive to depolarising agents (prolonged paralysis).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New difficulty swallowing, slurred speech, drooping eyelids, or shortness of breath.
  - New confusion, seizure, or headache with nausea.
  - Worsening weakness – especially unable to walk up stairs or brush hair.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urgent neurology, rheumatology, or oncology outpatient appointment (within 2 weeks).
  - Repeat imaging in 3–6 months if no primary found.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / anaesthetics / crash team if:**
  - SpO₂ <92% on room air, NIF <–30 cmH₂O, or single breath count <15.
  - GCS ≤12, new seizures, or Na <115 or Ca >3.5 mmol/L.
  - NEWS2 ≥6 (especially respiratory component ≥2 and rising).
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or pH <7.3 in setting of weakness.
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - Hypercalcaemia >3.0 → inform senior, call oncology.
  - Myasthenic crisis: any sign of hypoventilation → call ITU/pain team (neostigmine can be used but only after senior review).
  - SIADH with Na <120 → discuss with endocrine specialist before any fluid or DDAVP.
